speaker: interviewer
text: We have an interview with Dr. Vera Laska. Doctor Laska, when you were smuggling people over the borders, did you have false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We had false papers for most countries, just in case.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea where those were made or how they were made?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, well that was one part of the resistance, evidently, that manufacturered false papers, false ID cards, false passport, forged birth certificates, especially for Jews-- and what else-- ration cards, by the ton I'm sure, if you added it all together, because there were so many people in hiding. Don't forget it wasn't just the Jews who were running from persecution. But there were lots of people who were either part of the resistance or not part of the resistance, but suspected as part of the resistance. And they were persecuted too. So many of them went abroad. And many of them went into hiding, or lived under false-- with false identities.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the names that you used?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only name I really remember is Michalska, because I always liked that name. I don't know for what reason. And the others must have sort of come and gone, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some of the more eventful of the trips that you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, that I remember. Well, we-- we had some strange encounters, and we had some difficulties. For instance, people fleeing for their lives grab all kinds of strange things to lug with them, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: So one thing I remember is a couple who had silver candelabra. Very beautiful, I must say, if I remember correctly, and must have been very valuable, but very heavy. And we went on the mountains-- you know, uphill, downhill, uphill. And they just go, oh, I cannot carry it. Would you help me? And so then we decided that before we go, we will look what people have, and if it's silver candelabra, no way.

speaker: interviewee
text: One couple carried a teddy bear, which I still remember. Just a little teddy bear that doesn't weigh so much. One woman came in high heels, and we said, please change your shoes. Well, she doesn't have other shoes. Said you won't make it. And you cannot go shoeless because of stones and what have you, or it was winter or whatever. And we had trouble with the husband. He gave us hell, you know. And so I remember there was sharp exchange of words because he said something like, we are paying you, so you do as I said. And I said, no, you're not paying us. Nobody's paying us nothing. We are risking our necks, so no high heels. We didn't take them. We didn't take them. And somebody later somehow, I seem to remember, somebody else took them, but no high heels. I mean you go across the mountains in high-- and I mean high heels, beautiful high heels. So that was one part. And there's a sexy story in my book with the-- with the Frenchman. But I'm just indicating what happened then. I don't really know whether it happened, because I didn't hold a candle. But one of the Frenchmen and one of the guides-- we were waiting in the woods for time to pass to catch a certain train, not to be too early at the station. And they sort of disappeared in the woods. And I don't really know what happened. But-- so that's my sexy story in the book. The Frenchmen were fun. They were really fun. At one point, some Frenchman got stuck. I forget what happened to the line. And we had them for about a week. And it was amazing. We just took them, you know, to the coffee house, no papers. We must have been so stupid. And we took them skiing, and it was wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the Frenchmen trying to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the line went, as I mentioned, from Poland finally to Yugoslavia, from Yugoslavia by train to Beirut, Lebanon, which at that time was a beautiful place. I have never been there, but I got postcards saying we ski in the morning. We swim in the ocean in the afternoon. That remains so much in my mind-- such a miraculous place. And then, they went by ship on the-- in the Mediterranean to a city called Agde, A-G-D-E, in southern France. And of course, after France-- France fell, then the line avoided France, went directly to England. That's how it went. And of course, the Frenchmen first wanted to get home. And then after French-- France fell, they went to England. And there they had the choice. They didn't have to join de Gaulle's Free French. And that was the line, of course, through which the Czech boys went to join the Czech Army in-- Czechoslovakia army in exile in Great Britain. They were in British uniforms, but had Czechoslovakia here around the shoulder, an inscription, embroidered inscription. The same-- there were free Danes and free Norwegians, and free Poles, and I don't know what else. Most of the occupied countries had an army in exile or governments in exile. So that was the main purpose of these underground railroads.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the people that you smuggled prominent?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. You didn't-- you didn't ask names. And we were told two, and that's it. The Frenchmen, we knew somehow. If they gave us real names, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Czechs, there was one incident where the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Czechs were somewhat different because they were like brothers and sisters. But you forget. You forget names, you know, from one transport to another. At one part of the underground, I was stationed in Yugoslavia in Belgrade in the Czechoslovak house, so-called. And we had once or twice a week, a transport of anywhere from 25 to 50 young men, Czechs and Slovak, going to join the army against Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were very few names that I remember. But there were two boys I somehow remember more than anything else. And I think that I mentioned them in the-- in the book. And I think I told you over the phone such a miraculous thing happened just two days ago. Was it two days ago you called me up? Somebody sent me a clipping about them, about those two boys that I so well remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through the border together from Hungary to Yugoslavia, and I remember going around Belgrade with them. I knew them for three or four days, and then they left with the transport. And I had a couple of postcards and nothing else. They were 17 years old. Didn't finish high school. And the town from where these two boys were in the Czech part of the country, somehow remembered them at the 50th anniversary of-- I forget what this-- so many 50th anniversaries now-- and gave them honorary diplomas. And the two photographs are there, as they are already soldiers. And that was sort of-- I don't know. It-- it really shook me up, those two boys. And they both fell. In 1942, they were dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names? One was Jan Holub, and the other one was Jan-- I forget now. I should have the clipping. Would you like to see? Maybe when we change tapes. What were some of the dangers that you were up against on these trips?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the danger was to be caught by the border guard. But first of all, you know, you must realize this central European mishmash and the geographical move of the border. So you have suddenly Hungarian border guards on skis. You know, Hungary, the [NON-ENGLISH] flat like your hand? And so they were taught skiing in, I don't know, two easy lessons. And they didn't know from Adam how to ski. And we were-- several of us were racing skiers. You know, we could go right and left, and up and down. And we knew the terrain which was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were no match for us. Not mentioning that we were very careful to be told if somebody else was watching the border guards when they are patrolling. And whenever they were patrolling, we went five minutes right after, you see, and at night, so that we didn't have any trouble from the-- from those border guard. And if they would have caught us, we had these papers, as long as they didn't strip us, we could talk ourselves-- oh, I must mention that we all spoke both languages, fluent, no accents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is a very important part of the story. And maybe takes away a little bit from the heroism of the whole thing, because if you didn't speak the language, you were lost. Then you are suspicious. Then you are a foreigner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any close calls?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not-- not in this part of my activities. But I had a-- a scare, I had a terrible scare one night, when for some reason, which by now I don't remember. I was coming back from Hungary alone, because usually we were two or three together. This was alone. And it was dark, of course. And as I was crossing, let's say, from this place to the highway that you see there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I saw somebody standing next to that tree, maybe smoking a cigarette. For a moment I saw a flicker of light. And that person was standing there motionless. But I saw it's a soldier because it had a belt and something like a gun. And I stopped. And I didn't enter the meadow. There was a meadow-- forest, meadow, forest. And I was standing there, I'm telling you must have been eternity, probably three hours or so, not daring to move, because if I move, that soldier would shoot me. Didn't dare to run. Then finally, it became light, and there was nobody there. It was-- what I saw, the flickering must have been-- somebody explained to me later-- fluorescent. Rotting matter sometimes is fluorescent. And my imagination putting that together, it looked clearly, clearly like a soldier. And I was scared out of my wits. That was pretty horrible. And ever since, I said, I don't want to go alone. I want to go with somebody. It was really scary, because I knew if I move, I'll be shot. Nothing happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time that you've described, you were still working from your home town. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, more or less. Most of the time, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother aware of your activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. My mother would have fainted or done something like nice ladies do, hearing about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody know? Anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my friends knew who were in that group. Nobody else, no other friends knew, because we were told you keep your mouth shut. Can you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is your best friend? Would you tell him? No. And some of my best friends were in that group of five or six. Sometimes we got five, sometimes we got six. How did you receive your instructions? Who gave them to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: One in our group was the contact. I wasn't the contact. I had some very interesting experiences, aside from the smuggling in Belgrade, where we had this original center of gathering place of these young mostly men-- 99 out of 100 were men, and 1 woman-- who were fleeing the way I described to you through the Mediterranean to join the Czech army. We had all kinds of apartments rented for them because they had to wait two, three days in-- in Budapest or in Belgrade in the Czechoslovakian house. And in Budapest, while they were in these hired apartments, we didn't tell the Hungarians that they were Czechs because again, you have to know the central European mishmash. The Hungarians didn't like the Czechs, but they liked the Poles for historical reasons, you know. So we said these are Poles, Polish refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: So of course, the Hungarians didn't know the difference when they heard the language. Polish, Czech-- it was all the same to them. And so I would go from one apartment to another to check if everything is all right or take ration tickets or take medicine, or take somebody to the dentist or whatever. That's when I was a-- messenger, I assume would be the closest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at one place I arrived, and the boys are saying, oh, we are in trouble! We are in trouble! The landlady wants us to sing the Polish National Anthem and no idea what the Polish National Anthem. So, [LAUGHS] no problem, no problem. You all know the song, [NON-ENGLISH], 'The Gypsy Girl,' which was an old sort of, you know, a popular song. Yeah. So slow it down, make a sad face, and sing [NON-ENGLISH]. So we all stood there. The boys do this [INAUDIBLE] practically saluting. And we sang this popular song. I should come up with something equivalent in English-- some silly song, you know, like, [SINGING] how much is the doggie in the window-- sad face. The equivalent about the gypsy girl and her love. And the Hungarian landlady was satisfied. Beautiful Polish anthem. And the crisis was over. Yeah, I still remember that. And I was worried that the boys will, you know, burst out laughing or something, but they didn't. And then we went down Hungary to Yugoslavia. And that was the-- the most touching part, really, because the Yugoslavs knew about this underground rail. The border guards knew. And they were expecting us. And they were behind the border line, waiting for brother Czechs to come. And they took us to some-- you know, what do they have-- little house that the border guards have. And they had cots for us. And they had hot tea for us and food and blanket. And when we crossed into Yugoslavia, I remember I see-- I wish I could design-- I have two left hands-- this huge Yugoslav border-- border guard with his mustachios [BACKROUND NOISE] hanging down, pulled down to his ankles-- this is January, bitter cold-- and saying, welcome to freedom brother Czechs! You know, I could cry now when I think of it. And now look what they're doing in Yugoslavia now. You know, it's very sad. It's very sad.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was wonderful because the Hungarians were paid guides-- guides. And they betrayed us at least on that occasion, if not more. And they said, see those woods? That's Yugoslavia. So-- and then they left us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You go alone, you know, over the meadow to the woods, which was Yugoslavia, which was a lie. It was still Hungary. But they were afraid to go so close to the border to pay the guards. So we went to the woods and started hollering, hey, we made it! We made it! We were still in Hungary. And then there was a wire that they said, watch out! There's a wire. You have to step over it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stepped over the wire, but of course we moved the wire. And that wire was ringing at the Yugoslav border patrol, whatever you call it, house or barracks, or whatever. It was just a two-room shack affair. And so they knew-- oh, here they are. And that's when they put on their coats and came out and said, welcome to freedom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very interesting-- I wish I could-- I wished after the war to get a hold of some of those Hungarian guides who really tricked us. They took the money and left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of places did you live in? And who did you live with in the various places that you've stopped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Belgrade, it was very simple, because the Czechs had their own house. You know, there was a Czech ethnic minority in Yugoslavia. And they put, you know, their money together, and they actually built a house which had a big hall for dances or theater or whatever. And there, instead of the dance hall, they put cots down for the boys. And there were a couple of rooms-- I don't know-- might have been dressing rooms or what. And there were about 20 of us girls at that time in the Czechoslovak house. That, I remember so well, and the address and the number, and went back after the war. Of course, all the employees were gone, and the other people by that time. But I remember the-- the-- the street and the number. So that's where I lived. And in Budapest, I was one-- in one of those rented apartments with some other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the address in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the address in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Address?

speaker: interviewer
text: The address that you recall.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, not in Budapest, but in the Czechoslovak house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh! [SPEAKING SERBIAN] I still remember it in Serbian. Number 39 [NON-ENGLISH] Street. It's still-- at least it was still there a few years ago when I was there. I don't know how many Czechs lived there. Now-- now, everything is different. I was there before-- I mean, still under Tito. .

speaker: interviewer
text: How many trips total do you think that you made?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was often trying to figure it out-- definitely under two dozen and definitely more than 12. So pick your number in between. I don't know. It's so frustrating to me. I feel that I am sort of a historian. And I don't remember the most important-- some of the most important dates in my own life. At that time I couldn't care less. I don't know-- I don't know the date when I liberated myself or the name-- name of the village. I don't see how I could have been so stupid not to write it down or something. Do not remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to put an end to these activities? Or what happened to you next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened to me next? Next happened that somebody, evidently, spoke or talked when they were not supposed to. So that our-- our group was endangered. And out of the six of us, three somehow smelled the rat and disappeared, saved their necks. And three were taken to concentration camps. That's what happened. So it was very important not to talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that, you were living in Belgrade, is that right? And you lived through the bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: April 6th, that I remember because that was Easter Sunday. And the Saturday before, we went out on the town. We went to some, not night club, but sort of a wein-- weinstube, if you know what that is. You order a glass of wine and stay for hours for the price of that wine. You could dance.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went home. It must have been two o'clock at night. And as we were walking on the street-- there were about six of us or more-- we saw a sort of army marching in the direction of out of town. And one of us knew, evidently, a soldier, because they were shouting back and forth, hey, where are you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the soldier was saying, we're the last ones out of Belgrade. Belgrade was declared an open city several days before that, which meant no bombardment. So the thousands, tens of thousands of Serbians, especially women and children from the surrounding area, came to Belgrade to stay wherever they could because it's an open city. And that also means by international law all military must pull out. So he said, we are the last ones out of Belgrade.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went home. We went to bed. And I woke 10 minutes before 7:00 that Easter Sunday. And I had a little balcony at my rented room. And I looked out from that balcony, and at that moment I saw a bomb going boom about, I would say, half a mile from us where was the-- the newspaper political-- the biggest paper in Yugoslavia. And that, pssh, went up.

speaker: interviewee
text: The open city, by Nazi standards-- they bombed Belgrade to smithereens, to smithereens. So-- but they rebuilt it after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe your arrest? Where were you when it happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was very simple as a matter of fact, because they caught me on the street. ID, please. They looked at the ID and said, that's a false ID, which I knew, of course. And we were taken to the-- I was taken to the police. And there I met my two friends for a few moments.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, this was where? In which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Kosice, yeah. And we were in jail for several weeks, not many-- less than a month. And then this one day they just took us-- in a car, as a matter of fact, I don't know where they got the car-- to the railway station and stuck us in the train and that was it. And we ended up in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that train like going to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you must have read so many times about cattle cars and what have you. And we took about three days. It's all along Slovakia, and then a piece of Poland. The interesting thing about Auschwitz and the location, 'everybody' quote, unquote, knows that Auschwitz is in Poland, but the irony is that Auschwitz, at that time, was in Germany because of the movement of the borders, and because the Germans annexed Western Poland to-- to the Reich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Poland was only the general government in the middle. So-- what was the question? How was the arrival? Talk about the train ride.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the train, yes. Well, no food, no toilet facilities, barely a place to sit down, although the train had some compartments, you know, for humans, but that probably was taken up by the German police. I don't know. I didn't go through the train. But where we were, it was pretty miserable and smelly and dark, and people screaming and crying and cursing, but mostly no food and no water. And it was very shameful. Don't forget, I was a very young girl. And I was ashamed to go to the bathroom in front of all kinds of people I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in the car with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other resisters, smugglers, black marketeers, you name it-- the scum. We were part of the scum. And the-- the arrival at Auschwitz was like a phantasmagorical picture that you read in-- I don't know. is a fairy tale, compared with it, though. And it was dark. It was night. Or it was too early in the morning, so that it was dark. And you saw the fires going out of the chimneys. Not smoke-- you didn't see the smoke because of that, but you saw the fire. For a long time after the war, when I went somewhere or drove somewhere, and there was a factory-- an innocent factory-- with the fire coming out the chimney, I always remember that first moment in Auschwitz and the stink-- cannot call it smell-- stink as if hair was burning or flesh was burning, I don't know. And the shouting. It was a madhouse. And the zebras, I don't-- the zebras. It-- it was-- I'm sure everybody was in shock. I'm sure. And this German standing there, with his riding crop-- it must have been Mengele. We didn't know-- and dividing people, just like when the Jewish transports arrived-- same thing. For all I know, the train might have been a whole Jewish train with one additional, you know. Like Eichmann said, if you ever read the Eichmann's reports, he would say, I'm sending so many cars of Jews and attached is another car of gypsies, or and attached is another car-- he never said political prisoners-- vagabonds, or [NON-ENGLISH]-- what's the word in English-- anyhow, professional no-goodniks, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop you there for a tape change.
